claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 14 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child's age.**

How old is the child you're claiming for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, we can go through each one - just let me know!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my two children. Alex is 8 years old and Blake is 14. They both live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great that you're looking into Child Benefit for both Alex (8 years old) and Blake (14 years old). Since both children are under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit.

Let me continue with the assessment. Since both children are under 16, the next step is the same for both of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about work status. 

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given their ages (8 and 14), I would expect neither of them is working 24+ hours per week, but I just need to confirm this to proceed.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them work at all. They're just kids in school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes perfect sense! Since both Alex and Blake are in school and not working, they meet this requirement too. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are both Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means they are not personally claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit themselves. This is usually the case for children living at home with a parent.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits themselves. They're just regular kids living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming that. Let's continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake in any kind of apprenticeship?**

Since you mentioned they're both in school, I expect the answer is no - but I just need to confirm. 

For reference, most apprenticeships would disqualify a child from Child Benefit (such as apprenticeships in England or Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland), with the exception of Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales which are approved.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are doing any apprenticeships. They're just at school like I said - Alex is in primary school and Blake is in secondary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you for confirming. Since neither Alex nor Blake are in an apprenticeship, let's move on to the next check which will be about your residency status.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these to qualify:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - This is the most common situation
2. **Have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ months)
4. **Have pre-settled status** and actively seeking work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. **Have pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and family
6. **Have pre-settled status** and studying with sufficient resources
7. **Have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. **Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Do any of these apply to you? Most commonly, if you normally live in the UK, that will satisfy this requirement.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester with the kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements. Let's continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to confirm your responsibility for the children.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You meet this requirement if **ANY** of the following apply:

1. **You live with the children** - This is the most common situation
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** - Paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

You mentioned earlier that both children live with you at home in Manchester, so it sounds like you meet the "living with the children" requirement. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - they both live at home with me, so I'm responsible for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's confirmed. Since Alex and Blake both live with you, you meet the responsibility requirement. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about any other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent
- A former partner
- A grandparent or other family member

There are three possible situations:
1. **No one else is claiming** - We can proceed
2. **Someone else is claiming but you've agreed that you should receive it instead** - We can proceed
3. **There's a dispute about who should claim** - HMRC would need to make the decision

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's straightforward. Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake, we can continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about where the children are currently living.

**What is the current living situation for Alex and Blake?**

- **Living at home normally** - The typical situation
- **In hospital** - Special rules may apply
- **In local authority care** - Special rules may apply
- **In a foster care arrangement** - Special rules may apply

You mentioned they live at home with you, so I assume they're in a normal living situation - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - they're both just living at home with me normally. Nothing special about their situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thanks for confirming. Let's proceed with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! Just one final check.

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you fully supporting the children yourself without the local council contributing to their housing or upkeep costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - the council isn't paying anything towards them. I'm supporting them myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful news! That completes our assessment, and I can confirm that you appear to be eligible for Child Benefit for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit

Based on our assessment, you are **eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

### Summary

| Child | Age | Eligible? |
|-------|-----|-----------|
| Alex | 8 | ✅ Yes |
| Blake | 14 | ✅ Yes |

### Why You Qualify

Both children meet all the eligibility requirements:
- ✅ Under 16 years old
- ✅ Not working 24+ hours per week
- ✅ Not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✅ Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- ✅ You normally live in the UK
- ✅ You are responsible for them (they live with you)
- ✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them
- ✅ The local council is not paying towards their care

### Next Steps to Claim

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for Alex and Blake

### Important Information

- **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain other benefits)
- ⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or a partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of it back through your tax return. You can still claim and receive the benefit, but keep this in mind.

### Useful Link
👉 [How to Claim Child Benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (8 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not working 24+ hours per week; (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) They are not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together; (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living at home normally; and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (14 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not working 24+ hours per week; (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) They are not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Blake as they live together; (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (8) Blake is living at home normally; and (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. They are a UK resident living in Manchester with both children (Alex, age 8, and Blake, age 14) living at home with them. Both children are under 16, in school, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeships, and the local council is not contributing to their care. No other person is claiming Child Benefit for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit

Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

Blake (14 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not working 24+ hours per week; (3) They are not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) They are not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Blake as they live together; (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake; (8) Blake is living at home normally; and (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.
